content.text: Stacks and Queues: Ordered Collections with Controlled Access

Two of the most fundamental data structures in computer science, stacks and queues, solve a deceptively simple problem: how do you store a collection of items and control the order in which they are retrieved? While arrays and lists let you access any element at any position, stacks and queues enforce strict rules about which element comes out next. This constraint is not a limitation — it is the entire point. By restricting access, they become powerful tools for managing sequential processes, tracking state, and coordinating work.
is a collection where the last item added is the first item removed. This is called
. Think of a stack of plates: you place a new plate on top, and when you need one, you take from the top. You never reach into the middle.
is a collection where the first item added is the first item removed. This is called
. Think of a line at a coffee shop: the first person to join the line is the first person served. New arrivals go to the back.
Both structures expose a small, deliberate interface:
Neither structure lets you access items by index. You can only interact with one end (stack) or both ends (queue).
Real software constantly needs to track sequences of events, defer work for later, or reverse a chain of actions. Stacks and queues provide the exact semantics needed for these patterns:
are natural for anything that needs to "undo" or "go back" — function calls, browser history, expression parsing.
are natural for anything that needs to process items in the order they arrived — print jobs, task schedulers, network packet handling.

Without these abstractions, you would have to manually manage indices, track front and back pointers, and enforce ordering rules yourself — and you would likely introduce bugs. Stacks and queues encode the ordering rule into the data structure itself.
A stack maintains a single pointer to the "top" of the collection. Every operation touches only this top:
: Place a new item on top. The top pointer advances.
: Remove the item at the top. The top pointer retreats.
: Look at the top item without removing it.

Initial:   []
Push 10:   [10]         ← top
Push 20:   [10, 20]         ← top
Push 30:   [10, 20, 30]         ← top
Pop:       [10, 20]          ← top  (returns 30)
Pop:       [10]              ← top  (returns 20)
A stack can be implemented using either a
Both give O(1) push and pop. The array version has better cache locality; the linked list version avoids occasional resize costs.
: Add a new item at the rear.
: Remove the item at the front.
: Look at the front item without removing it.

Initial:      []
Enqueue 10:   [10]          front=10, rear=10
Enqueue 20:   [10, 20]      front=10, rear=20
Enqueue 30:   [10, 20, 30]  front=10, rear=30
Dequeue:      [20, 30]      returns 10
Dequeue:      [30]          returns 20
A naive array-based queue is inefficient — dequeuing from the front requires shifting all remaining elements (O(n)). The standard solutions are a
makes the array wrap around like a ring, avoiding the need to shift elements.

STACK (LIFO)
─────────────────────────────────────
  push(A) → push(B) → push(C)

  ┌───┐
  │ C │  ← top (most recent)
  ├───┤
  │ B │
  ├───┤
  │ A │  ← bottom (oldest)
  └───┘

  pop() returns C, then B, then A


QUEUE (FIFO)
─────────────────────────────────────
  enqueue(A) → enqueue(B) → enqueue(C)

  front                        rear
    ↓                            ↓
  ┌───┬───┬───┐
  │ A │ B │ C │
  └───┴───┴───┘

  dequeue() returns A, then B, then C
All core operations are O(1). This is what makes stacks and queues so efficient as building blocks.
something (reverse a string, reverse a path)
(the call stack itself is a stack)
Do NOT use a stack or queue when:
by index — use an array or list instead
— use a hash table or sorted structure
— use a heap or priority queue instead
— use a binary search tree or sorted list
in O(1). It generalizes both a stack and a queue.

from collections import deque

d = deque()
d.append(1)        # add to right
d.appendleft(0)    # add to left
d.pop()            # remove from right
d.popleft()        # remove from left
is implemented as a doubly-linked list of fixed-size blocks, giving O(1) operations on both ends with good cache performance.
is a queue where dequeue returns the highest-priority item, not the oldest. It is backed by a
(covered separately), not a simple array. Every enqueue and dequeue costs O(log n).

import heapq

pq = []
heapq.heappush(pq, (2, "medium priority task"))
heapq.heappush(pq, (1, "high priority task"))
heapq.heappush(pq, (3, "low priority task"))

print(heapq.heappop(pq))  # (1, 'high priority task')

maintains elements in strictly increasing or decreasing order. When a new element violates the order, elements are popped until the invariant is restored. This is useful for solving "next greater element" problems in O(n).

Every browser maintains a stack of visited URLs. When a user navigates to a new page, the current page is pushed. When the user clicks Back, the top page is popped and restored. The Forward button is a second stack that accumulates pages popped from the Back stack.

Back stack:    [google.com, amazon.com, reddit.com]  ← top
Forward stack: []

User clicks Back:
  Back stack:    [google.com, amazon.com]
  Forward stack: [reddit.com]

User navigates to new page (twitter.com):
  Back stack:    [google.com, amazon.com, reddit.com, twitter.com]
  Forward stack: []    ← forward history is cleared
Every programming language runtime uses a stack to track function calls. When a function is called, a
is pushed containing local variables and the return address. When the function returns, its frame is popped and execution resumes at the caller.

main() calls foo()
foo() calls bar()

Call stack:
  ┌──────────┐
  │  bar()   │  ← currently executing
  ├──────────┤
  │  foo()   │
  ├──────────┤
  │  main()  │
  └──────────┘

bar() returns → frame popped → back in foo()
foo() returns → frame popped → back in main()
error occurs when recursive calls push so many frames that the stack runs out of memory.

A web server handling incoming HTTP requests uses a queue to manage them. Requests are enqueued as they arrive and dequeued by worker threads as capacity becomes available. This ensures requests are handled in arrival order and prevents any single burst of traffic from dropping requests.

Incoming requests:  [R1, R2, R3, R4, R5]   (enqueued as they arrive)

Worker threads dequeue and process:
  Thread 1 takes R1
  Thread 2 takes R2
  Thread 1 finishes R1, takes R3
  ...
all use queue semantics at their core.
A classic stack application: given a string of brackets like
, determine if they are properly nested and closed.
This works because the most recently opened bracket must be the next one closed — a perfect LIFO scenario.
Popping from an empty stack or queue.
. Failing to do so causes runtime errors or undefined behavior.
Using a plain Python list as a queue.
dequeues from the front, but it shifts all remaining elements, making it O(n). Use
instead, which gives O(1) on both ends.

# Wrong — O(n) per dequeue
queue = []
queue.append(1)
queue.pop(0)   # shifts all elements!

# Correct — O(1) per dequeue
from collections import deque
queue = deque()
queue.append(1)
queue.popleft()
Confusing a priority queue with a regular queue.
in Python is not a FIFO queue — it orders by priority. Do not use it when you need arrival-order processing.
Every recursive call uses a stack frame. Unbounded recursion on large inputs will exhaust the call stack. For deep traversals, convert recursive algorithms to iterative ones using an explicit stack.
Forgetting to handle the empty case in peek.
Peeking an empty stack or queue should raise an error or return a sentinel value — not silently return
Implementing a fixed-size queue without circular buffering.
A simple array queue that moves the front index forward wastes all the space before the front pointer. Use a circular buffer or a linked list to avoid this.
for both stacks and queues. It is implemented in C, handles edge cases, and is thread-safe for

from queue import Queue   # thread-safe FIFO queue
q = Queue(maxsize=10)
q.put(item)
item = q.get()
If a component only needs stack behavior, do not expose the full underlying array. Wrap it in a class with only
. This prevents accidental misuse and makes the intent clear.
Choose implementation based on use case.

For a stack or queue whose maximum size is known in advance, a circular array gives better cache performance than a linked list. For unbounded growth, a linked list or dynamic array avoids the need to predict capacity.
. The names communicate ordering semantics to future readers.
When recursion is elegant but the input can be large (deep trees, long paths), convert the recursion to a loop with an explicit stack. This avoids call stack limits and gives better performance.

# Recursive DFS — risks stack overflow on deep trees
def dfs_recursive(node):
    if node is None:
        return
    process(node)
    dfs_recursive(node.left)
    dfs_recursive(node.right)

# Iterative DFS with explicit stack — safe for any depth
def dfs_iterative(root):
    stack = [root]
    while stack:
        node = stack.pop()
        if node is None:
            continue
        process(node)
        stack.append(node.right)
        stack.append(node.left)
For single-threaded use, both stacks and queues offer O(1) operations regardless of size — they scale linearly in memory and constant in time.

uses locks internally to make operations thread-safe, but lock contention becomes a bottleneck under high concurrency. For high-throughput systems, lock-free queues (like those backed by ring buffers using atomic compare-and-swap) are used.
In distributed systems, queues are implemented as
: RabbitMQ, Apache Kafka, AWS SQS, and Redis Lists all implement queue semantics at scale, supporting millions of messages per second with persistence, replication, and consumer groups.

is particularly notable: it implements a distributed, append-only log that behaves like a persistent queue with multiple independent consumers, each tracking their own position. This allows one queue to fan out to many downstream consumers without losing messages.
Distributed task queue for async jobs

Recursive algorithms that do not have proper base cases or that run on adversarially deep inputs can exhaust the call stack. Mitigate with explicit stack-based iteration, recursion depth limits, or tail-call optimization where the language supports it.
In a task queue serving multiple priority levels, low-priority tasks can be permanently delayed if high-priority tasks continuously arrive. Mitigate with
(gradually increasing the priority of waiting tasks) or by reserving a fraction of processing capacity for lower-priority queues.
A queue with a fast producer and a slow consumer will grow without bound, eventually exhausting memory. Always set a maximum queue size in production systems, and decide upfront whether to
in Python is not safe for concurrent push/pop from multiple threads without locks. Use
or proper synchronization primitives in concurrent code.

as a spring-loaded plate dispenser in a cafeteria. Plates are pushed down by the spring as you add them. When you take a plate, the spring pops the top one up to you. You always interact with the top.
as a ticketing line at a counter. People join at the back and are served from the front. No cutting in line — the first person to arrive is the first person served.

These mental models reveal the structural constraints: stacks have one active end, queues have two. Both enforce their ordering rule absolutely, which is precisely what makes them predictable and useful.
follows LIFO (Last In, First Out) — only the top element is accessible, making it ideal for reversals, undo operations, and depth-first traversal.
follows FIFO (First In, First Out) — the front element leaves first, making it ideal for scheduling, buffering, and breadth-first traversal.
All core operations — push, pop, enqueue, dequeue, peek — run in
in Python for both stacks and queues; avoid
for queue dequeues as it is O(n).
enables O(1) enqueue and dequeue in a fixed-size array queue by reusing vacated space.
are powerful extensions that solve "next greater element" and "sliding window maximum" problems in linear time.
In production systems, distributed queue brokers like
implement queue semantics at massive scale with persistence and fault tolerance.
